reason: Both answers are well-structured, correctly diagnose the fragility risk, offer similar concrete technical mitigations (remote config for selectors, stable ytd-* anchors, breakage telemetry, MV3 review latency), and both call out diversification as the strategic fix. Key differentiators: (1) Capturing users into a direct relationship (accounts/email) is one of the four key points. A states this explicitly ("get accounts/emails early so a broken extension doesn't mean losing the relationship"); B only gestures at "own the relationship directly" via a new surface, missing the explicit account/email capture point. (2) On worry level, A gives an unambiguous "very worried — top business risk," matching the intended message, while B hedges into "moderately worried about breakage," resting partly on a speculative, unsupported claim (gradual rollout gives 1-2 weeks warning) that could give false reassurance. (3) B does add a useful extra angle (ranking DOM churn vs. Chrome policy vs. deliberate blocking/ToS enforcement), a genuine plus, but it doesn't fully offset the gaps above. On balance A better nails the key points asked for.
